query: association between chronic obstructive pulmonary disease and multifocal atrial tachycardia

retrieved_docs.title: Multifocal Atrial Tachycardia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Multifocal atrial tachycardia (MAT) is a supraventricular tachycardia resulting in a rapid, irregular atrial rhythm that is caused by multiple ectopic foci within the atria. This arrhythmia is characterized by a heart rate of greater than 100 beats per minute with organized atrial activity yielding three or more different non-sinus P-wave morphologies in the same lead. There are irregular intervals between P waves. This condition is typically seen in elderly patients with a variety of underlying conditions, the most common of which is a chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). While the pathogenesis is not well understood, the condition is generally asymptomatic, and most patients are hemodynamically stable. For the majority of patients, no treatment is required beyond the treatment of underlying conditions. However, evaluation is important as this arrhythmia is a poor prognostic sign in the setting of acute illness. This activity describes the presentation, causes, diagnosis and treatment of multifocal atrial tachycardia and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in the management of affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Multifocal Atrial Tachycardia -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Multifocal atrial tachycardia is most commonly associated with underlying pulmonary disease, cardiac disease, electrolyte abnormalities (mainly hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia), drugs and chronic renal failure. This arrhythmia is associated with significant lung disease in 60% of cases and has been found in 20% of patients with acute respiratory failure and 17% of patients hospitalized with COPD. It has also been associated with conditions such as coronary artery disease, congestive heart failure, diabetes, the post-operative period after major surgery, pulmonary artery hypertension and the use of medications such as aminophylline, theophylline, and isoproterenol. [4] [5] [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Multifocal Atrial Tachycardia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Multifocal atrial tachycardia (MAT) is a supraventricular tachycardia with a rapid, irregular atrial rhythm arising from multiple ectopic foci within the atria. This arrhythmia is characterized by a heart rate of greater than 100 beats per minute with organized atrial activity yielding three or more different non-sinus P-wave morphologies in the same lead. There are irregular PP intervals and an isoelectric baseline between P waves (Figure 1). This condition is typically seen in elderly patients with a variety of underlying conditions, the most common of which is a chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). While the pathogenesis is not well understood, it is generally asymptomatic, and most patients are hemodynamically stable. For the majority of patients, no treatment is required beyond the treatment of underlying conditions. However, evaluation is important as this arrhythmia is a poor prognostic sign in the setting of acute illness. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Multifocal Atrial Tachycardia -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Decompensated chronic lung disease Coronary artery disease Heart failure Valvular heart disease Diabetes mellitus Hypokalemia Hypomagnesemia Postoperative state Pulmonary embolism Pneumonia

retrieved_docs.title: Multifocal Atrial Tachycardia -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: In the absence of underlying pulmonary disease, the first-line agent is beta-blockers. A beta-blockers act to suppress ectopic foci by reducing sympathetic stimulation and decreasing conduction through the atrioventricular node, thereby slowing the ventricular response.  Studies have found an average decrease in heart rate of 51 beats per minute and 79% of patients reverted to sinus rhythm.  Most patients did not need beta-blocker therapy long term as studies found long-term therapy was needed in only 25% of patients.  Caution should be used in patients with an underlying pulmonary disease such as COPD and patients with decompensated heart failure due to the increased risk for bronchospasms and decreased cardiac output.  Furthermore, beta-blockers should be avoided in patients with atrioventricular blocks unless a pacemaker has been implanted.

retrieved_docs.title: Paroxysmal Atrial Tachycardia -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with atrial tachycardia can be asymptomatic or symptomatic. If asymptomatic, the patient’s arrhythmia will be found incidentally on an EKG or any rhythm recorder. Patients who present symptomatically may complain of dizziness, palpitations, dyspnea, lightheadedness or chest pain. The patient’s atrial tachycardia could be a manifestation of an underlying condition. Patients must be worked up for underlying pathologies. For example, multifocal atrial tachycardia is most commonly elicited by an underlying chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (60%). The hypoxemia and increased bronchodilator use, which in turn causes increased catecholamine release, is thought to initiate multifocal atrial tachycardia.

retrieved_docs.title: Arrhythmias -- Clinical Significance -- Tachyarrhythmia
retrieved_docs.content: Management: General goals include control of ventricular rate with AV blocking agents (beta-blockers or calcium channel blockers), but the restoration of sinus rhythm through cardioversion or ablation is preferred. Multifocal Atrial Tachycardia (MAT) Mechanism: multiple automatic atrial foci due to increased sympathetic tone secondary to various causes, including hypoxemia (chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), or stimulant use. Signs & Symptoms: Usually asymptomatic. Patients will have symptoms of the underlying illness, such as dyspnea. EKG Findings: Three or more P wave morphologies with different PR intervals. Management: Oxygen therapy if hypoxemic and treatment of the underlying cause. Refractory cases: Rate Control with calcium channel blockers as the first choice in the setting of COPD followed by beta-blockers. Junctional tachycardia: [7] Arrhythmia originating from or near the AV node. Mechanism: Rhythm arising from the AV node.

retrieved_docs.title: Multifocal Atrial Tachycardia -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The theory of abnormal automaticity focuses on an increase in the ability of atrial myocytes to spontaneously depolarize and trigger an action potential. This theory is supported by many of the underlying conditions associated with this arrhythmia. Pulmonary diseases, like COPD, can result in hypoxia, hypercapnia, acidosis, and increased adrenergic stimulation, all of which are known to increase automaticity. Furthermore, pulmonary hypertension associated with pulmonary diseases can result in right atrial enlargement and right atrial hypertension, which can also increase automaticity. Similarly, the ventricular dysfunction seen in coronary artery disease and congestive heart failure can result in atrial enlargement and atrial hypertension that can also increase automaticity. The electrolyte abnormalities and medications associated with this arrhythmia, are also known to increase automaticity. However, given all the above information the role of abnormal automaticity in multifocal atrial tachycardia has not yet been fully understood.

retrieved_docs.title: Multifocal Atrial Tachycardia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the causes of multifocal atrial tachycardia. Outline the presentation of a patient with multifocal atrial tachycardia. Review the treatment strategy for multifocal atrial tachycardia. Explain a well-coordinated interprofessional team approach to provide effective care to patients affected by multifocal atrial tachycardia. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Multifocal Atrial Tachycardia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Multifocal atrial tachycardia should be suspected in patients with tachycardia and irregularly irregular rhythm. Given its association with underlying medical conditions, it should also be suspected in patients with cardiac and pulmonary disease. Diagnosis is not clinical, but is made with electrocardiogram and can be made using the following diagnostic criteria. The electrocardiogram should show an atrial rate of greater than 100 beats per minute (although some suggest using a threshold of 90 beats per minute) with three or more discrete P wave morphologies in the same lead, not including that originating from the sinoatrial node. Furthermore, there should be irregular PP intervals, and the baseline should be isoelectric between P waves. Other findings that are commonly seen, but are not diagnostic include irregular PR and RR intervals. Variation in PR intervals has not been included in the diagnostic criteria because the PR interval varies with the length of the preceding RP interval. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Multifocal Atrial Tachycardia -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: While most patients with multifocal atrial tachycardia are hemodynamically stable, it is a poor prognostic sign in the setting of an acute illness. Studies have shown a 60% in-hospital mortality and mean survival of just over one year.

retrieved_docs.title: Multifocal Atrial Tachycardia -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Multifocal atrial tachycardia is most often asymptomatic. However, patients typically have symptoms related to their underlying condition. Therefore, this arrhythmia is often found incidentally on the routine electrocardiogram or on a cardiac telemetry monitor. Studies show most people do not report palpitations or symptoms of syncope or pre-syncope. Once a diagnosis is made, a thorough history should be obtained with a focus on commonly associated conditions including cardiac and pulmonary diseases. On physical exam, most patients will have an elevated heart rate and an irregularly irregular rhythm. Most patients are hemodynamically stable, however, due to the association with underlying conditions, it is sensible to conduct a general assessment for signs of cardiopulmonary disease, especially since this arrhythmia can trigger decompensation of underlying cardiac and pulmonary disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Multifocal Atrial Tachycardia -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Multifocal atrial tachycardia is a relatively uncommon arrhythmia seen in 0.05% to 0.32% of electrocardiograms in general hospital admissions. The average age of patients is approximately 70 years.

retrieved_docs.title: Atrial Tachycardia -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Focal atrial tachycardia typically occurs secondary to an underlying disease process or acute illness.

retrieved_docs.title: Multifocal Atrial Tachycardia -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: In the presence of underlying pulmonary disease, the first-line agent is a non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker such as verapamil or diltiazem. These agents act to suppress atrial rate and decrease conduction through the atrioventricular node, thereby slowing the ventricular rate.  Studies have found an average reduction in the ventricular rate of 31 beats per minute and 43% of patients reverted to sinus rhythm. Caution should be used in patients with preexisting heart failure or hypotension due to negative inotropic effects and peripheral vasodilation.  Similarly, calcium channel blockers should also be avoided in patients with atrioventricular blocks unless a pacemaker has been implanted.

retrieved_docs.title: Multifocal Atrial Tachycardia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: A diagnosis of multifocal atrial tachycardia does not typically warrant any additional workup, other than workup required for any underlying conditions. However, if arrhythmia persists despite the treatment of underlying medical conditions it may be worth checking a complete blood count and serum chemistry for signs of infection, anemia, or electrolyte abnormalities such as hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 4 -- Community 2870
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac dysrhythmias are classified under the broader category تَسَارُّعُ فَوقَ البُطَينِيّ and frequently co‑occur with رفرفة أذينية. Recognizing that cardiac dysrhythmias belong to the supraventricular tachycardia family and are often associated with atrial flutter helps clinicians anticipate related rhythm disturbances, guide diagnostic evaluation, and tailor therapeutic strategies for optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2747
retrieved_docs.content: Congestive cardiomyopathy (اعتلال عضلة القلب الاحتقاني) is clinically associated with both supraventricular tachycardia (تَسَارُّعُ فَوقَ البُطَينِيّ) and atrial flutter (رفرفة أذينية). These arrhythmic conditions frequently arise in the setting of congestive cardiomyopathy, reflecting a close pathophysiological link in which impaired myocardial function predisposes patients to rapid supraventricular rhythms and organized atrial flutter circuits. Recognizing this association is important for clinicians, as the presence of supraventricular tachycardia or atrial flutter may signal underlying congestive cardiomyopathy and guide diagnostic evaluation and therapeutic strategies aimed at both the structural heart disease and its rhythm complications.

Overall, the interconnections among transient cerebral ischemia, its transitory clinical course, cardiac electrophysiology studies, specific arrhythmic foci (atrial fibrillation and ventricular acceleration), associated diseases (rheumatic heart disease, transient ischemic attack), and medication contraindications highlight a comprehensive framework for understanding the diagnosis, evaluation, and management of related cardiovascular and cerebrovascular conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1769
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary artery rupture is linked to acute myocardial infarction (احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ الحادّ). Acute myocardial infarction is clinically associated with supraventricular tachycardia (تَسَارُّعُ فَوقَ البُطَينِيّ) and with rheumatic heart disease (مرض قلبي روماتزمي). The infarction also maps to coronary artery embolism (انصمام الشريان التاجي). In addition, supraventricular tachycardia is clinically associated with rheumatic heart disease, indicating a shared relationship between this arrhythmia and rheumatic cardiac involvement. Recognizing that coronary artery rupture can precipitate acute myocardial infarction, which frequently co‑occurs with supraventricular tachycardia and rheumatic heart disease and often involves coronary artery embolism, provides clinicians with an integrated framework for evaluating and managing patients with these interconnected cardiac conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2834
retrieved_docs.content: CARDIAC CONDUCTION DISEASE WITH OR WITHOUT DILATED CARDIOMYOPATHY is a cardiac disorder that can manifest as اعتلال عضلة القلب الاحتقاني, and this form of congestive cardiomyopathy is clinically associated with رفرفة أذينية (atrial flutter). Recognizing that patients with this conduction disease may develop congestive cardiomyopathy and subsequently present with atrial flutter is important for comprehensive cardiac assessment and timely management of arrhythmic complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2348
retrieved_docs.content: Electrophysiologic evaluation with arrhythmia induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, treatment of supraventricular tachycardia is a diagnostic and therapeutic approach that specifically targets تَسَارُّعُ فَوقَ البُطَينِيّ (supraventricular tachycardia). Clinical observations indicate that تَسَارُّعُ فَوقَ البُطَينِيّ is frequently associated with مرض قلبي روماتزمي (rheumatic heart disease), underscoring the need to assess for underlying rheumatic cardiac disease when managing patients with supraventricular tachycardia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 576

Collectively, the interconnections among acute myocardial infarction, various forms of heart muscle injury, coronary arterial thrombotic events, associated arrhythmias, and related systemic conditions illustrate the need for integrated diagnostic and therapeutic strategies in cardiovascular medicine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 896
retrieved_docs.content: Acute myocardial infarction (احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ الحادّ) is closely interconnected with several cardiac conditions. It is clinically associated with supraventricular tachycardia (تَسَارُّعُ فَوقَ البُطَينِيّ) and with rheumatic heart disease (مرض قلبي روماتزمي), indicating that patients with rheumatic involvement of the heart may be more prone to both ischemic events and rapid supraventricular rhythms. The condition is also related and possibly synonymous with coronary artery occlusion (انسداد الشريان التاجي), reflecting that an acute blockage of a coronary vessel is a central pathophysiologic feature of the infarction. Further, acute myocardial infarction maps to specific mechanisms of coronary obstruction, namely coronary artery embolism (انصمام الشريان التاجي) and coronary artery thrombosis (خثار الشريان التاجي), underscoring that embolic or thrombotic clot formation within the coronary circulation can precipitate the infarct. Supraventricular tachycardia itself is clinically associated with rheumatic heart disease, reinforcing the link between rheumatic valvular pathology and arrhythmic complications. Together, these relationships highlight the importance of assessing coronary artery patency, embolic and thrombotic risk, and underlying rheumatic heart disease when diagnosing and managing acute myocardial infarction and its associated arrhythmias.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2024
retrieved_docs.content: The electrophysiologic evaluation with arrhythmia induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, treatment of atrial fibrillation; the electrophysiologic evaluation with arrhythmia induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, treatment of supraventricular tachycardia; and the electrophysiologic evaluation with arrhythmia induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, treatment of ventricular tachycardia are all classified as دراسة فيزيولوجية كهربائية قلبية, representing cardiac electrophysiology studies that deliberately induce arrhythmias, identify arrhythmogenic foci, and apply catheter ablation to treat the respective rhythm disorders. In the atrial fibrillation procedure, the primary focus is الرجفان الأذيني, whereas the ventricular tachycardia procedure targets التسارع البطيني, which is a subtype of نظمٌ بُطَينِيٌّ ذاتِيٌّ مُعَجَّل (self‑accelerated ventricular rhythm). Clinically, الرجفان الأذيني is associated with مرض قلبي روماتزمي, indicating a link between atrial fibrillation and rheumatic heart disease. Pharmacologically, both drospirenone and estetrol monohydrate are contraindicated in patients with الرجفان الأذيني, underscoring the need to avoid these agents in individuals with atrial fibrillation. Together, these relationships highlight the diagnostic and therapeutic role of cardiac electrophysiology studies, the specific arrhythmic targets they address, the disease associations of atrial fibrillation, and important medication contraindications that inform safe patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2377
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac conduction disease with or without dilated cardiomyopathy is linked to multiple cardiac manifestations, including cardiac dysrhythmias and اعتلال عضلة القلب الاحتقاني (congestive cardiomyopathy). Cardiac dysrhythmias are clinically associated with تضخم البطين الأيسر (left ventricular hypertrophy) and frequently co‑occur with رفرفة أذينية (atrial flutter). In addition, اعتلال عضلة القلب الاحتقاني is clinically associated with both رفرفة أذينية and تضخم البطين الأيسر. These interconnections indicate that patients with this conduction disorder may present with rhythm disturbances, ventricular hypertrophy, and atrial flutter, emphasizing the importance of comprehensive cardiac assessment and integrated management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1776
retrieved_docs.content: CARDIAC CONDUCTION DISEASE WITH OR WITHOUT DILATED CARDIOMYOPATHY is characterized by the manifestation of cardiac dysrhythmias and also by the manifestation of اعتلال عضلة القلب الاحتقاني (congestive cardiomyopathy). The cardiac dysrhythmias that occur in this condition are clinically associated with تضخم البطين الأيسر (left ventricular hypertrophy), frequently co‑occur with رفرفة أذينية (atrial flutter), and are categorized under the parent rhythm disorder تَسَارُّعُ فَوقَ البُطَينِيّ (supraventricular tachycardia). Likewise, اعتلال عضلة القلب الاحتقاني shows clinical associations with تَسَارُّعُ فَوقَ البُطَينِيّ, رفرفة أذينية, and تضخم البطين الأيسر, indicating a close interrelationship among these electrophysiological and structural cardiac abnormalities. Recognizing these linked manifestations and associations is important for comprehensive evaluation and management of patients with cardiac conduction disease and related cardiomyopathic changes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2557
retrieved_docs.content: The electrophysiologic evaluation with arrhythmia induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, treatment of atrial fibrillation and the electrophysiologic evaluation with arrhythmia induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, treatment of supraventricular tachycardia are both classified as a دراسة فيزيولوجية كهربائية قلبية (cardiac electrophysiological study). Each procedure involves deliberately inducing arrhythmias, identifying the arrhythmogenic focus, performing ablation of that focus, and delivering therapeutic treatment targeted to a specific rhythm disorder—atrial fibrillation in the first case and supraventricular tachycardia in the second. As forms of cardiac electrophysiological study, they provide essential diagnostic insight and therapeutic options for managing abnormal heart rhythms.
